subject: UX & Research
title: From Medicine to Marketing
description: A fast breakdown of From Medicine to Marketing for builders.

hook.text: In the 1920s, direct mailers sent different letters to random households. Response rates differed wildly.

buildup.text: Claude Hopkins wrote 'Scientific Advertising' in 1923. He argued that advertising should be measured, not guessed.

discovery.text: Coupon codes tracked which ad version drove sales. This was A/B testing before computers existed.

twist.text: Most advertisers ignored Hopkins for decades. They trusted creative instinct over data until the internet arrived.

climax.text: The web made testing cheap and instant. What took months with mail now takes hours with digital experiments.

punchline.text: Testing isn't new. The internet just made it fast and cheap.

quiz.question: How did early marketers conduct A/B tests before the internet?
options: Sending different mail versions and tracking responses with coupon codes
Running focus groups in shopping malls
Placing different ads on different TV channels
